mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Hungary?
mapQuestions.answer: Budapest. It used to be two cities — Buda on one bank, Pest on the other — joined together in 1873.

mapQuestions.question: What is the famous river that runs through Budapest?
How many countries border Hungary?
mapQuestions.answer: Seven: Austria, Slovakia, Ukraine, Romania, Serbia, Croatia, Slovenia.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Great Hungarian Plain called?
mapQuestions.answer: The Puszta — a huge flat grassland in the east, like a European prairie.

foods.name: Goulash
foods.description: A rich beef and paprika stew. Hungary's national dish, served in a deep bowl with bread.

foods.name: Paprika
foods.description: Ground sweet red pepper — Hungarian cooking uses it in almost everything. Sweet, smoky, sometimes spicy.

foods.name: Lángos
foods.description: Deep-fried flatbread topped with garlic, sour cream, and cheese. Street food at its best.

foods.name: Kolbász
foods.description: Hungarian smoked sausage, usually with paprika and garlic. Eaten with bread and pickles.

foods.name: Chimney cake (Kürtőskalács)
foods.description: Sweet dough wrapped around a cylinder and grilled until caramelised. Like a crunchy spiral pastry.

foods.name: Dobos torte
foods.description: A layered sponge cake with chocolate cream and a hard caramel top. A Budapest classic since 1884.

scavengerItems.label: The Parliament building on the Danube
Strings of dried red paprika hanging outside a shop
One of Budapest's big bridges over the Danube
A bakery with chimney cake spinning over a grill
A yellow tram clanking through the city
A folk-music musician playing accordion or violin
A street name with at least 12 letters

geoMatches.description: The capital. Grand parliament, eight bridges across the Danube, thermal baths everywhere.
Central Europe's biggest lake. Hungarians call it "the Hungarian Sea" — no coast, this is the next best thing.
The Great Plain. Flat grassland in the east where cowboys (csikós) still ride horses and herd cattle.
Old wine town in the north. Famous for "Bull's Blood" red wine and a castle that held off the Ottomans for weeks.
A national park on the Puszta. Famous for grey cattle, racka sheep with twisted horns, and stork nests on chimney tops.

senses.smell: Paprika, woodsmoke from outdoor grills, sweet pastry, river water, thermal sulphur in bath districts…
senses.hear: Tram bells, the Danube against river boats, accordion and violin in restaurants, "Szia!" greetings…
senses.taste: Smoky paprika, sour cream on everything, crispy chimney cake, dense rye bread, cold pálinka (fruit brandy)…
senses.feel: Steamy hot springs even in winter, cobbles under your feet, scratchy folk-embroidered tablecloths…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why does Budapest have so many thermal baths?
stories.body: Budapest sits on more than 100 natural hot springs that bubble up from deep underground. The Romans first built baths here 2000 years ago, then the Turks built dome-roofed ones in the 1500s, then the Habsburgs built grand spa palaces in the 1800s. Today the city has dozens of public thermal baths, and Hungarians of all ages soak in them — kids splash in the warm pools, grandparents play chess on floating boards. The Széchenyi Baths are open even when it's snowing.

stories.question: Who were the Magyars, and why don't Hungarians speak a language like their neighbours?
stories.body: Most European languages come from a few big families — Romance (Italian, French, Spanish), Germanic (German, English), or Slavic (Polish, Russian). Hungarian isn't any of those. It comes from a totally different family with cousins only in Finland and Estonia, brought to Europe in 895 AD by a tribe called the Magyars who rode in from the Ural Mountains far to the east. Hungarians still call themselves "Magyar". Their language has no genders, no future tense, and 18 grammatical cases — fiendishly hard to learn but it makes them very proud.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your country's language wasn't spoken anywhere else, what would be good about it? What would be hard?

stories.question: Why is there a Hungarian wine called "Bull's Blood"?
stories.body: In 1552 the Ottoman army of 80,000 soldiers tried to capture the castle of Eger, defended by just 2,000 Hungarians (including women fighting alongside men). The Hungarians drank lots of strong red wine to keep their courage up — when the Ottomans saw their red-stained beards they thought the Hungarians were drinking bull's blood for strength. Eger held out, the Ottomans gave up, and the wine has been called "Bull's Blood" (Egri Bikavér) ever since.

stories.question: What was the 1956 uprising?
stories.body: After WWII, Hungary was controlled by the Soviet Union (a communist state run from Moscow). In October 1956, Hungarian students started protests demanding freedom. The protests grew into a revolution — for 12 days Hungarians overthrew the communist government and tasted freedom. Then the Soviet Union sent tanks. Around 2,500 Hungarians were killed and 200,000 fled the country. Hungary stayed under Soviet control until 1989. The revolution failed but it inspired the whole world — Time Magazine named the Hungarian Freedom Fighter their "Person of the Year".
stories.thinkingQuestion: Why might a country risk a revolution they know they might lose?

stories.question: Why does Hungary have cowboys?
stories.body: The Great Hungarian Plain (Puszta) is so flat and open it's perfect for cattle and horses — like the American Wild West but 1000 years older. Hungarian csikós (cowboys) traditionally wore blue shirts and felt hats, herded grey longhorn cattle, and trained horses to lie down on command. A few still work the plains today. The traditional skill of doing the "Puszta Five" — standing on the backs of two horses while controlling five at once — is still taught at Hungarian riding schools.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Hungary so far?
Hungarian thermal baths — relaxing or just weird? Would you go again?
The Danube runs through 10 countries. Have you crossed it on a bridge here?
If we could move one Budapest building to your home town, which would it be?
What's one Hungarian thing you'd like to bring home?
Hungarian is famously hard to learn. Have you tried saying anything yet?

wordSearch.hint: Hungarian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Nesting on chimneys, electricity poles, and church towers across the countryside.

animals.name: Hungarian grey cattle
animals.description: Iconic long-horned cattle on the Puszta plains. Hungary's national breed.

animals.name: Racka sheep
animals.description: Twisted, spiral-shaped horns up to a metre long. Found mainly on the Hortobágy plains.

animals.name: Vizsla
animals.description: Russet-coloured Hungarian hunting dog — one of the country's most loved breeds.

animals.name: Wild ducks on the Danube
animals.description: Mallards, pochards, and tufted ducks along the river right through Budapest.

animals.name: Saker falcon
animals.description: Hungary's national bird, nesting on castle ruins and farm buildings.

animals.name: Eurasian beaver
animals.description: Returned to Hungarian rivers in the 1990s after centuries of absence.
